english: *lán ldóṅ-cè* (lan ldong ce) to take vengeance, to revenge one's self.

W. 1. to make warm, to warm, mé-la (me la) at the fire, e. g. one's hands, a plate. — 2. to light, to kindle, *me dū́g-cè* (me dug ce) to light a fire; *káṅ-pa mes dug soṅ* (khang pa mes dug song) the house has begun to burn, has caught fire; *zá-cè dug tsár-kán* (za ce dug tsar khan) burnt food, a burnt meal; *dū́g-ḍi* (dug ḍi) a burnt smell.

1. a tortoise shell, duṅ-rdó (dung rdo) a petrified tortoise shell Cs. — 2. a shell, both small shells, worn as an ornament (skye-duṅ-pṛéṅ (skye dung phreng) necklace of shells), and more particularly the great trumpet-shell, which is sounded on certain occasions; it is usually of a pure white, hence duṅ-dkár (dung dkar) 1. trumpet-shell, 2. white rose C., duṅ-so (dung so) snow-white teeth Pth, duṅ-ru (dung ru) snow-white horns Mil.; a trumpet-shell wound to the right (ryás-su ₒkyíl-ba (ryas su 'khyil ba)) is regarded as valuable as it is rare Glr. — 3. trumpet, tuba, duṅ ₒbúd-pa (dung 'bud pa) to sound, to blow a trumpet; kṛíms-duṅ (khrims dung) judgment-trumpet, trumpet used in courts of justice, cǒs-duṅ (chos dung) church-trumpet, trumpet used in religious ceremonies, dmag-duṅ (dmag dung) war-trumpet, líṅs-duṅ (lings dung) hunting-bugle; rkáṅ-duṅ (rkang dung) a trumpet or cornet made of a hollow thigh-bone; zaṅs-duṅ (zangs dung) a copper trumpet, a bass tuba eight feet long; dbaṅ-duṅ (dbang dung) a similar instrument, but of less dimensions; rwa-duṅ (rwa dung) a trumpet of horn, rag-duṅ (rag dung) a brass trumpet. — 4. skull (?) Sch. has: duṅ-cén (dung chen) 1. skull, 2. = rkaṅ-duṅ (rkang dung); in Glr. Brahma is called duṅ-gi tor-tsogs-càn (dung gi thor tshogs can).
constant, continual Dom.; duṅ-ṅe-ba (dung nge ba) Thgr. id.

secondary form of ydúṅs-pa (gdungs pa), love, dád-pa daṅ dū́ṅs-pa źig (dad pa dang dungs pa zhig) skyés-te (skyes te) Mil., frq.; yid-dū́ṅs (yid dungs) = snyíṅ-brtse-ba (snying brtse ba), frq.; *dhúṅ-bhu* (dhung bhu) C. love, *ṭú-gu-la dhúṅ-bu ǰhê-pa* (ṭu gu la dhung bu jhe pa) cf. ycéś-pa (gces pa).

I. sbst. (cf. dú-ba (du ba), and the Pers. دود) smoke, W.: *káṅ-mig dúd-pa mā méd-kaṅ dug* (khang mig dud pa ma med khang dug) there comes very little smoke into the room. — dúd-k'a (dud kha) Sch. 1. having the colour of smoke, dark-gray. 2. family, household. 3. chimney (?). — dúd-ku (dud khu) Sch. 'liquid soot'; prob. soot mixed with water, smut; Lt. compares morbid evacuations or matter ejected from the stomach with dud-ku (dud khu). — dud-bál (dud bal) soot Sch., prob. flocky soot. — dud-bún (dud bun) a cloud of smoke Cs. — dud-rtsi (dud rtsi) soot, smut Cs. — dud-lám (dud lam) chimney. II. vb. 1. to tie, to knit, to knot, v. mdúd-pa (mdud pa), — 2. pf. of ₒdúd-pa ('dud pa), stooping, bent, hence dúd-ₒgro (dud 'gro) quadruped, beast, animal, opp. to man that walks erect Stg.
great diligence, assiduity, dún-pa drág-po (dun pa drag po); *dún-càn* (dun can) very diligent W. (cf. ₒdún-pa ('dun pa), and rtun (rtun)).

vb. to be or get tired; adj. tired; sbst. fatigue; mi dúb-bo (mi dub bo) they do not get tired Dzl.; ṅál-źiṅ dúb-nas (ngal zhing dub nas) Glr.; lus daṅ ṅag yid dub (lus dang ngag yid dub) Pth. he is tired in body, mouth, and soul, i.e. he has no strength for doing, saying, or thinking anything good. — dúb-càn (dub can) tiresome Cs. — dúb-rgyu (dub rgyu) anxious, sorrowful Sch.
Stg. frq.: nyé-źiṅ dubs nyé-bar (nye zhing dubs nye bar) acc. to the context it might mean: very probably; but the word seems to be little known.

a piece, frq.; as a measure or certain quantity of meat, v. yzúgs (gzugs); dúm-po (dum po) a large piece Cs.; dúm-bu (dum bu) a small piece, frq.; dúm-bur ɣcóg-pa (dum bur gcog pa), ɣcód-pa (gcod pa), byéd-pa (byed pa) to break, to cut to pieces. — dam-dúm (dam dum) several small pieces or things Cs.; perh. = dum-dúm (dum dum) Ld., e.g. yul dum-dúm (yul dum dum), or groṅ dum dúm (grong dum dum) several scattered farms, hamlets or villages, which have together one common name.

tomb, grave, dúr-du ₒǰúg-pa (dur du 'jug pa), dzúd-pa (dzud pa) (Cs. ₒdébs-pa ('debs pa)) *(s)kúṅ-cè* (skung ce) W., to bury; dur rkó-ba (dur rko ba) to dig a grave. — dúr-rkun (dur rkun) grave-robber, plunderer of tombs. — dúr-kuṅ (dur khung) grave, tomb. — dúr-krod (dur khrod) acc. to etymology denotes a cemetery, burial-
